one_line_takeaway: A TEE remote-attestation wrapper that cryptographically proves a specific open-source guardrail was executed to produce an agent's response, keeping the agent private at ~34% latency overhead, while the authors emphasize this proves execution, not safety.
key_finding: The system is end-to-end feasible with modest cost: TEE execution adds roughly 25-38% latency overhead (~34% on average) over a non-TEE baseline, attestation generation adds only ~98ms and verification ~5ms. All three simulated tampering attacks are detected during verification (guardrail-code modification -> measurement mismatch; attestation-byte modification -> invalid signature; response modification -> commitment-hash mismatch). Crucially, the paper's own accuracy numbers show the underlying guardrails are imperfect (e.g. Llama Guard 3 unsafe-class F1 only 0.56), so the authors argue proof-of-guardrail must NOT be interpreted or advertised as proof-of-safety.
core_contribution: The paper proposes 'proof-of-guardrail', a system that lets an AI-agent developer produce a cryptographic proof that a given response was generated after a specific, named open-source guardrail was executed. The proof is created by running a wrapper program f (which bundles the public guardrail g and mediates all agent I/O and tool calls) inside a Trusted Execution Environment (TEE); the TEE emits a hardware-signed remote-attestation document binding the enclave measurement m (a hash of f) and a commitment d = Hash(x, r) over the input x and response r. Any user can verify this offline without the developer disclosing the proprietary agent A. The authors are explicit that the contribution is proving guardrail EXECUTION, not improving guardrail accuracy, and they deliberately frame the work as 'what (not) to trust': a valid proof establishes execution, not safety.
threat_model: A remotely deployed agent is owned by a developer (the prover P) who publicly claims to run a specific guardrail g. The intended pipeline is r = g(x, A) for user instruction x, where A is the developer's private agent. A negligent or malicious developer may instead return r' generated without g, or with a modified/misconfigured g, i.e. safety measures are falsely advertised. The user is the verifier V who wants to confirm g actually ran. Desiderata: (1) computational integrity of guardrail execution, (2) confidentiality of the proprietary agent A, (3) input x and output r are NOT assumed confidential from the developer (who mediates the user-agent channel). Trust is anchored in the cloud/hardware root (AWS Nitro hypervisor to correctly measure code and protect TEE keys), avoiding any additional trusted third-party auditor. The paper is candid about residual threats: because g must be open-source, a malicious developer can jailbreak g and still present a valid proof; and vulnerabilities in the measured program f (e.g. arbitrary command execution by the un-measured agent A inside the enclave) could bypass the guardrail.
limitations: (1) Proof of execution is not proof of safety: guardrails make errors (imperfect precision/recall in Table 3) and, because g is open-source, a malicious developer can jailbreak it while still presenting a valid proof. (2) The measured wrapper f must itself be free of vulnerabilities that let the un-measured agent A bypass g (e.g. arbitrary command execution inside the enclave); the suggested mitigation is restricting late-injected agent A to non-executable prompt artifacts. (3) Trust is shifted to, not eliminated at, the cloud/hardware root (AWS Nitro) and to external LLM/tool APIs whose trust is left to the verifier. (4) High infrastructure cost: the enclave must hold the full runtime in memory, forcing larger instances (18.5x hourly cost vs a t3.micro). (5) Defining 'best-practice' open-source guardrails is left to an unspecified community process. (6) Evaluation scale is small (single instance, two guardrails, limited attack set) and the attestation-skill variant omits input x from the commitment.

datasets_used: ToxicChat (content-safety guardrail inputs, via Llama Guard 3-8B)
FacTool-KBQA (factuality guardrail inputs, via Loki fact-verification tool)
quantitative_results: Average TEE latency overhead ~34% vs non-TEE deployment (per-task range 25-38%)
Llama Guard 3 execution latency: 546.7+/-223.2 ms (TEE) vs 421.2+/-246.7 ms (non-TEE baseline), +29.7%
Response generation on ToxicChat: 2828+/-1663 ms (TEE) vs 2050+/-531 ms (baseline), +38.0%
Loki fact-check latency: 20408+/-12115 ms (TEE) vs 15964+/-9582 ms (baseline), +27.8%
Response generation on FacTool-KBQA: 2408+/-729 ms (TEE) vs 1930+/-472 ms (baseline), +24.8%
Attestation generation latency: 97.8+/-4.2 ms (~100ms additional); user-side verification: 5.1 ms
Attack detection: guardrail code modified -> measurement m mismatch, 10/10; attestation byte modified -> signature invalid, 100/100; response r modified -> Hash(x,r) mismatch, 100/100
Cost: m5.xlarge $0.192/hr vs t3.micro non-TEE $0.0104/hr = 18.5x higher
Llama Guard 3 content-safety accuracy: Safe P=0.87/R=0.89/F1=0.88; Unsafe P=0.59/R=0.54/F1=0.56
Loki fact-check accuracy: Non-Factual P=0.71/R=0.81/F1=0.76; Factual P=0.74/R=0.61/F1=0.67
baselines_compared: Non-TEE (regular) deployment latency baseline for guardrail execution and response generation
Cost baseline: t3.micro non-TEE instance ($0.0104/hr) vs m5.xlarge TEE instance ($0.192/hr)

Attestable audits (Schnabl et al., 2025) discussed as the most relevant prior TEE-based approach, contrasted for flexibility (adopting validated guardrails without rerunning full audits) rather than empirically benchmarked
